title: The Hero

jokes.text: Are you really pushing my drink back? Encroachment. - Encroachment? Yes, you're encroaching. Oh. Sorry. That's a penalty. Passage or penalty?
jokes.setup: Minor airplane etiquette dispute over armrest space
jokes.punchline: Larry treating it like a football penalty with 'passage or penalty'
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - turning mundane social friction into pseudo-official terminology. The football reference is unexpected and character-specific.

jokes.text: Look at this. Look at the size of these laces. Have you noticed that they're making laces much longer than they used to? I'm just-- I'm actually trying to read something.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to make conversation about shoelaces
jokes.punchline: Passenger politely shutting him down mid-observation
jokes.explanation: Sets up the shoelace theme that becomes central to the episode's premise. Larry's obliviousness to social cues is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: This bathroom is for coach passengers only. Really? Who said that? Um, earlier I tried to use the one up there and I was told that, you know, coach isn't allowed to use first class.
jokes.setup: Coach passenger trying to enforce bathroom segregation
jokes.punchline: Larry's incredulous response to airplane class system
jokes.explanation: Great setup for the extended airplane bathroom class warfare sequence. Larry's bewilderment at petty rule enforcement is very in-character.

jokes.text: But I'm not like a first-class person. I'm coachy. It's just that you're not acting coachy. You're not acting coachy. You're acting first classy.
jokes.setup: Larry defending his down-to-earth nature despite first class seat
jokes.punchline: The invented terms 'coachy' and 'first classy' as personality descriptors
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that captures the absurdity of class-based personality assumptions. 'Coachy' and 'first classy' are perfectly crafted neologisms.

jokes.text: Poor misunderstood first class. Oh, poor little coachy girl. She's so jealous 'cause everybody else is more comfortable than the coachy girl. I just want to pee somewhere-- Everywhere. I have nothing.
jokes.setup: Class warfare escalating into melodrama
jokes.punchline: The coach passenger's breakdown into existential despair over bathroom access
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from petty dispute to existential crisis. The transition from bathroom access to 'I have nothing' is absurdly dramatic.

jokes.text: I said scotch with two ice cubes. No water. I cannot serve you any more alcohol. What? For Christ's sake, I'll get it myself. Where's the drink cart?
jokes.setup: Drunk passenger being cut off by flight attendant
jokes.punchline: His increasingly irrational demands including self-service
jokes.explanation: Standard drunk passenger escalation, but necessary setup for Larry's 'heroic' moment. The specific drink order adds character detail.

jokes.text: Oh no, I'll stop. Please don't hurt me. Don't hurt me. Don't hurt me. I'll go back to my seat. Please don't hit me. I-- I just-- it was-- Don't hit me anymore. I didn't really--
jokes.setup: Drunk passenger being aggressive and threatening
jokes.punchline: Instantly cowering and pleading when Larry accidentally falls on him
jokes.explanation: Great reversal from aggression to cowardice. Sets up the central premise of Larry being mistaken for a hero through accident.

jokes.text: That was you? That was me. I jumped on him. Crazy. What motivated you? You know, uh, he was abusing the stewardess and I just did it.
jokes.setup: Larry being praised for his heroic intervention
jokes.punchline: Larry accepting credit while we know it was accidental
jokes.explanation: Larry's willingness to accept undeserved credit is perfect character work. The irony drives the episode's central premise.

jokes.text: Look at you. How about that? So not like you. I didn't even think about it really. God, you're very brave. Huh. You are. You're a hero. That too, I suppose.
jokes.setup: Donna praising Larry's bravery and heroism
jokes.punchline: Larry's casual 'That too, I suppose' response to being called a hero
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry understatement while basking in undeserved glory. The casual acceptance of heroic status is beautifully written.

jokes.text: What is the deal with these shoelaces now? They make them so long. I mean, I'm tripping over these laces five times a day. But you got a girl out of it.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about long shoelaces again
jokes.punchline: Jeff pointing out the ironic benefit of Larry's clumsiness
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that connects Larry's earlier complaint to the central plot. The irony of his problem becoming a benefit is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Only you and I know you weren't an intentional hero. You don't think there's any way in the world I would have done something about that guy? Impossible.
jokes.setup: Jeff calling out Larry's accidental heroism
jokes.punchline: Jeff's flat 'Impossible' to Larry's suggestion he might have acted intentionally
jokes.explanation: Jeff's blunt assessment of Larry's character is perfectly delivered. The certainty in 'Impossible' is brutally honest and funny.

jokes.text: Ricky Gervais is sitting in the booth behind you. Oh, really? Can I turn around or is that-- All right, are we ready?
jokes.setup: Jeff telling Larry about celebrity behind them
jokes.punchline: Waiter interrupting at precisely the wrong moment
jokes.explanation: Good awkward timing with the waiter's interruption. Sets up the celebrity encounter that drives the restaurant storyline.

jokes.text: Would you like me to get a bottle of wine for Mr. Gervais? Sure. How did you-- Okay. Yeah, sure. Please. Excellent. I'll take care of that.
jokes.setup: Waiter overhearing conversation about Ricky Gervais
jokes.punchline: Larry agreeing without knowing he's being set up for expensive wine
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the expensive wine payoff. The waiter's eavesdropping and Larry's oblivious agreement work well together.

jokes.text: See, you've got to be careful with these waiters. They come over to your table, they eavesdrop. I think that's why they're always pouring the water. You can't say anything personal. I think that's one of the perks of the job is picking up the tidbits. It's a tidbit festival.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining waiter eavesdropping behavior
jokes.punchline: Describing it as 'a tidbit festival'
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry observation with perfect phrasing. 'Tidbit festival' is a memorable and quotable way to describe gossip collection.

jokes.text: Did he order the wine? Yes, he got a very nice bottle. In fact, it was a $300 bottle. So the best in the house. A $300 bottle of wine? Are you serious?
jokes.setup: Larry asking about the wine he ordered for Ricky
jokes.punchline: Revelation of the outrageous $300 price
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the wine setup. Larry's shock at the price is earned and relatable, setting up future conflicts.

jokes.text: Look. He looks very happy.
jokes.setup: Larry watching Ricky Gervais enjoy the expensive wine
jokes.punchline: Larry's bitter understatement as he watches money being enjoyed
jokes.explanation: Good understated reaction beat, economical and character-appropriate bitterness

jokes.text: My blood sugar is just going down to my feet. If I don't eat something-- I feel, like, faint. Our food's sitting up there? Yeah. Well, what the fuck? What is he doing? He's just sitting there schmoozing with them?
jokes.setup: Jeff dramatically describing his hunger
jokes.punchline: Larry's indignation at their delayed food service
jokes.explanation: Good buildup to Larry's restaurant rule-breaking. Jeff's medical dramatics paired with Larry's outrage work well.

jokes.text: How about if I just go get it? If you do that you're a hero. Really? You're a hero. You got it, pal.
jokes.setup: Larry suggesting he get their food himself
jokes.punchline: Jeff calling him a hero for basic self-service
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the hero theme. The contrast between actual heroism and getting your own food is perfect Larry logic.

jokes.text: Enjoying your meal? As a matter of fact I am. Um, where did this food come from? I brought it here. You understand I'm the waiter? Yet we were the ones who were doing the waiting. So we became the waiters.
jokes.setup: Waiter confronting Larry about self-service
jokes.punchline: Larry's twisted logic about becoming waiters themselves
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry logic turning customer service on its head. The wordplay with 'waiting' and 'waiters' is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: This man is a hero. He just revolutionized the way restaurants work, my friend. No one's gonna go hungry again.
jokes.setup: Jeff defending Larry's self-service
jokes.punchline: Elevating food fetching to revolutionary status
jokes.explanation: Great escalation connecting minor rule-breaking to world-changing revolution. The grandiosity is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: I wonder what Sully would have done if he was in my shoes? Not that I'm comparing myself to Sully.
jokes.setup: Larry wondering about Sully Sullenberger's hypothetical actions
jokes.punchline: Immediately claiming he's not making the comparison while obviously doing exactly that
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry self-aggrandizement with immediate false modesty. The construction is flawless and very quotable.

jokes.text: Once I went to the seventh game of a world series and came home on the subway and it was very crowded and hot and disgusting. People were sweating on me. But I maintained my composure. I thought that was a good sign.
jokes.setup: Larry describing his previous 'test' of character
jokes.punchline: Treating subway ride endurance as evidence of heroic potential
jokes.explanation: Great character work showing Larry's delusions of heroic potential. The mundane 'test' contrasted with his self-importance is perfect.

jokes.text: No wine? Everybody brings wine. I bring bread. It's Italian bread. Yeah, but this doesn't go with my menu. You don't know what I'm serving. Don't impose your desires on my whole cuisine.
jokes.setup: Larry bringing bread instead of wine to dinner party
jokes.punchline: Host's dramatic objection to bread disrupting his menu vision
jokes.explanation: Good host neurosis paired with Larry's contrarian gift choice. The phrase 'impose your desires on my whole cuisine' is nicely overwrought.

jokes.text: Oh my God, I can't believe this. Signed. The funniest show in the history of television. It was funny. I wouldn't call it the funniest show in the history of television.
jokes.setup: Host gushing over signed Seinfeld DVD from Ricky
jokes.punchline: Larry's modest deflation of the praise for his own show
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment with Larry modestly disagreeing with praise for his own creation. Shows character consistency.

jokes.text: It's just odd to just show up with your own DVD. Yeah. Never heard of somebody who's in something and then showing up with it. You've never heard of that? No, it's an odd thing.
jokes.setup: Larry questioning Ricky bringing his own DVD as gift
jokes.punchline: The awkwardness of self-promotion disguised as generosity
jokes.explanation: Great social observation about the narcissism of gifting your own work. Larry's directness in calling it odd is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: If you don't mind my saying... Not at all. It looks slightly affected. Yeah, I was worried about that. But one person thinking it's affected, 1,000 people let down...
jokes.setup: Larry commenting on Ricky's scarf looking affected
jokes.punchline: Ricky's cost-benefit analysis of appearing pretentious vs disappointing audiences
jokes.explanation: Good balance between Larry's bluntness and Ricky's performer logic. The mathematical approach to social perception is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Was it? I didn't look at the price. You didn't? No, not when it's a gift. That's kind of odd, isn't it? No, not when it's a gift. Really? Does it? It ruins it. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Larry pressing Ricky about not checking the wine price
jokes.punchline: Ricky's philosophy about gift etiquette vs Larry's transactional thinking
jokes.explanation: Good exploration of gift-giving psychology. Shows the cultural divide between Larry's money consciousness and proper etiquette.

jokes.text: I've separated the couples. What? I separated the couples. Why? What's the point of that? Meet new people, mingle. I don't want to meet new people. I don't want to mingle.
jokes.setup: Host announcing separated couples seating arrangement
jokes.punchline: Larry's blunt rejection of forced socialization
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry antisocial response to dinner party convention. His directness about not wanting to mingle is wonderfully honest.

jokes.text: You know, when I first saw you I guessed you for a spaniard. Yeah. I'm glad you're not Spanish. Really? I'm not a fan of the Spanish. Why is that? It's a personal thing. I got picked on a lot by some kids. By spaniards? Yeah. Well, they spoke Spanish.
jokes.setup: Hank revealing his prejudice against Spanish people
jokes.punchline: The illogical childhood trauma basis for ethnic discrimination
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from casual observation to absurd prejudice. The logical disconnect between Spanish speakers and Spain is perfect.

jokes.text: What is that? I'm sorry, I've shattered your bread. What is that? It's not my bread. Larry brought it. It's hard, huh? Wow. It's supposed to be hard. Not this hard.
jokes.setup: Guest trying to break Larry's hard Italian bread
jokes.punchline: Bread literally shattering instead of breaking normally
jokes.explanation: Good physical payoff to the bread gift setup. The extreme hardness validates the host's earlier objection in an unexpected way.

jokes.text: I'm trying to elevate small talk to medium talk. Okay, fine. It's all right. You know, it's fine. It's okay. How often do you have sex? Almost never.
jokes.setup: Hank explaining his invasive questioning philosophy
jokes.punchline: Larry's blunt answer to extremely personal question
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and brilliant concept of 'medium talk.' Larry's matter-of-fact response to inappropriate intimacy is perfectly written.

jokes.text: You say you're separating the couples? You're not separated. We're the hosts. Oh, you're the hosts? You fixed this whole thing, okay? You fixed it all so you could be at the cool table.
jokes.setup: Larry questioning the host's seating arrangement hypocrisy
jokes.punchline: Accusation of manipulating seating to create a 'cool table'
jokes.explanation: Great Larry logic exposing social manipulation. The 'cool table' concept brings dinner party politics into sharp focus.

jokes.text: So sorry. Ricky, can I please pay you for that? Oh, don't. It's very expensive. It's okay, I insist. It's a $500 scarf. That's okay. I'm good for it.
jokes.setup: Guest offering to pay for damaged scarf
jokes.punchline: Revelation of another absurdly expensive price
jokes.explanation: Continues the running theme of unexpectedly expensive items. Sets up financial obligations that will complicate relationships.

jokes.text: Hank, what happened? I tripped over my shoelaces. That's exactly how Larry fell into the guy on the plane. What? On the plane he tripped over his shoelaces. Larry, is that true?
jokes.setup: Hank tripping over shoelaces at dinner
jokes.punchline: Connection revealed between Larry's heroic story and accidental nature
jokes.explanation: Excellent plot convergence revealing Larry's fraud through parallel accident. The structure is very well-crafted.

jokes.text: I don't have any choice. You're like an appendage to us.
jokes.setup: Larry saying not to invite him anymore
jokes.punchline: Host calling Larry an unavoidable appendage
jokes.explanation: Brutal and perfectly phrased dismissal. The medical terminology for unwanted attachment is wonderfully harsh.

jokes.text: They're charging me $200 for the ticket. I thought he was giving it to me for free. What can you do? What is it with this guy? He's costing me a fortune.
jokes.setup: Larry discovering he has to pay for 'free' theater ticket
jokes.punchline: Recognition of pattern of Ricky costing him money
jokes.explanation: Good buildup of Larry's mounting expenses. The pattern recognition adds to his mounting frustration.

jokes.text: I'm scrunched in here. I want that seat. Oh, fuck you, Larry. I'm the girl. Shut up. You're a girl? You're a witch.
jokes.setup: Theater seating dispute with gendered argument
jokes.punchline: Larry's blunt rejection of gender-based seating privilege
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with Larry's characteristically blunt response to social expectations. 'You're a witch' is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Shut the fuck up. If that is so, then I am done.
jokes.setup: Serious dramatic theater performance
jokes.punchline: Audience member shouting profanity during emotional scene
jokes.explanation: Great cringe moment with profanity disrupting high drama. The contrast between art and audience behavior is perfectly awkward.

jokes.text: She was a hideous hideous looking person. I don't think you got a good look, Larry. I got a great look. The woman was quite attractive if I recall. Maybe on some bizarro planet.
jokes.setup: Ricky describing the disruptive theater patron
jokes.punchline: Larry's harsh assessment and bizarre planet reference
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's willingness to be harsh about Susie. The 'bizarro planet' reference adds comic book absurdity.

jokes.text: Are you kidding? 'Oklahoma'? 'Oh what a beautiful morning, oh what a beautiful day. I've got a beautiful feeling, everything's going my way.' Oh dear. Oh, come on.
jokes.setup: Larry defending Oklahoma against Ricky's artistic pretension
jokes.punchline: Larry enthusiastically singing the optimistic show tune
jokes.explanation: Good cultural clash between high and popular art. Larry's earnest singing shows his democratic taste in entertainment.

jokes.text: 'Poor Jud is dead. Poor Jud is dead.' Please. Bubblegum.
jokes.setup: Continuing Oklahoma vs serious drama debate
jokes.punchline: Larry singing another Oklahoma song and Ricky's dismissive 'bubblegum'
jokes.explanation: Continues the cultural argument with Larry's democratic arts appreciation vs Ricky's elitism. The contrast works well.

jokes.text: No, they're real tears. Of course, real tears every night. Seriously? Come on. Where do you think they came from? Here's where they came from. You're not crying. There's no tears coming out.
jokes.setup: Ricky claiming he cries real tears in performance
jokes.punchline: Larry examining Ricky's face for evidence of crying
jokes.explanation: Great Larry skepticism leading to physical investigation. His forensic approach to tear verification is wonderfully literal.

jokes.text: At the will-call window I picked up the ticket and they charged me $200. You thought it'd be more? I thought-- You told me you were getting me a ticket. I did get you a ticket.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about paying for 'free' ticket
jokes.punchline: Ricky's literal interpretation of 'getting' a ticket vs giving one free
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay exploiting the ambiguity of 'getting' someone a ticket. Shows how people interpret favors differently.

jokes.text: That was a gift-- The gift of wine. Thank you. And I've given you the gift of art. Whether you give me a gift or I give you a gift, it costs me money.
jokes.setup: Exchange about gifts and their costs
jokes.punchline: Larry's realization that all 'gifts' in this relationship cost him money
jokes.explanation: Excellent logic revealing the one-sided nature of their exchange. Larry's economic analysis of friendship is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: Oh well, I was just really trying to elevate small talk to medium talk. That's all I was doing. Well, don't.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining his invasive dinner conversation
jokes.punchline: Ally's flat rejection of his conversational innovation
jokes.explanation: Great callback to the earlier 'medium talk' concept. Ally's blunt shutdown is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: And another thing, I fuck my husband four or five times a week. Four or five times a fucking week. So shove that up your ass.
jokes.setup: Ally defending her marriage against Larry's implications
jokes.punchline: Explicit sexual frequency declaration with aggressive delivery
jokes.explanation: Explosive payoff to the sex frequency question. The aggressive repetition and crude ending make this a standout moment.

jokes.text: I owe you $500, Larry says you owe him $500. So he wants me to give the $500 to him directly. Is that cool? No good? Not really.
jokes.setup: Hank proposing Larry's debt consolidation scheme
jokes.punchline: Ricky's flat rejection of the obvious scam
jokes.explanation: Good setup and deflation of Larry's opportunistic scheme. Ricky's understated rejection works well.

jokes.text: I know it was you two idiots whispering all the way through my show. I wasn't anywhere near him. What are you talking about? Where do you get that from? The waiter.
jokes.setup: Ricky accusing Larry and Jeff of theater disruption
jokes.punchline: The revelation that the gossipy waiter was the source
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the eavesdropping waiter setup. The connection between restaurant and theater creates satisfying plot convergence.

jokes.text: It's like Laurel and Hardy. Donna was right. What? What did you say? Nothing. No, you just said Donna was right. Yeah, she is. So you've been talking to Donna.
jokes.setup: Ricky comparing Larry and Jeff to comedy duo
jokes.punchline: Accidental revelation of talking to Larry's love interest
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with accidental revelation. The Laurel and Hardy comparison is apt for Larry's bumbling partnerships.

jokes.text: Give me your money. Don't move! Okay okay. Give me that ring! Give me that ring! Now! Come on! Give me that! Take 'em-- Take 'em off! Just give him your shoes. Take them off!
jokes.setup: Mugger robs the group
jokes.punchline: Larry's practical advice to just comply with all demands
jokes.explanation: Sets up the actual heroic moment. Larry's practical cowardice is in character but not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: Shut up! Okay, don't hit me in the face though.
jokes.setup: Violent mugging in progress
jokes.punchline: Larry's specific concern about facial damage during robbery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry vanity even during life-threatening situation. His specific concern about face damage shows character priorities.

jokes.text: Yeah, I was just about-- Whoa. Yeah, well. I believe this belongs to you, young lady. My God, Larry. There you go. What do you know?
jokes.setup: Larry appearing after mugging with recovered purse
jokes.punchline: His casual heroic gesture and Donna's amazement
jokes.explanation: Good redemption moment where Larry becomes an actual hero. The casual delivery contrasts nicely with the heroic act.

jokes.text: Oh, and you're right about the bread, Simmington. It is hard.
jokes.setup: Larry walking away from heroic moment
jokes.punchline: Casual acknowledgment of dinner party bread complaint
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-ending callback with impeccable timing. The mundane bread acknowledgment after heroic rescue is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: Hey! Hey! No no! Hey! Hey! Hey! Hey! Hey!
jokes.setup: Larry trying to chase the muggers
jokes.punchline: His ineffective pursuit and repeated shouting
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy ending with Larry's futile heroic gesture. Works as episode closure but isn't particularly innovative.

tmdb_overview: Larry plays the hero in the sky and underground; Jeff’s courtship of Ricky Gervais is sabotaged by a nosy waiter.
